perturbation.original_text: Affiliate agrees to place Chase's links provided by Linkshare NetworkTM which manages the Affiliate Program ("Links") on its Affiliate's website. Affiliate is responsible for obtaining prior written approval from the Chase Affiliate manager or Linkshare Network to link any or all other sites owned or managed by the Affiliate, other than the site that was approved at the time of original registration.
perturbation.changed_text: Affiliate agrees to generally consider placing Chase's links provided by Linkshare NetworkTM which manages the Affiliate Program ("Links") on its Affiliate's website. Affiliate may attempt to obtain approval from the Chase Affiliate manager or Linkshare Network to link any or all other sites owned or managed by the Affiliate, other than the site that was approved at the time of original registration.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates the placement of Chase's links and requires prior written approval for linking other sites. The modified text weakens these obligations by using 'agrees to generally consider' and 'may attempt to obtain approval,' making compliance discretionary rather than mandatory. This introduces ambiguity and makes the obligations difficult to enforce.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code § 17500 prohibits false or misleading advertising. By making the placement of links and the need for prior approval discretionary, the modified text allows for potential ambiguity and misrepresentation of the affiliate's relationship with Chase, potentially misleading consumers if links are not placed or are placed without proper authorization. This can be considered a form of deceptive advertising, violating the statute's core principle of truthful representation in advertising.

Advertising [17500 - 17606] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 63. ) ARTICLE 1. False Advertising in General [17500 - 17509] ( Article 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 63. ) 17500. It is unlawful for any person, firm, corporation or association, or any employee thereof with intent directly or indirectly to dispose of real or personal property or to perform services, professional or otherwise, or anything of any nature whatsoever or to induce the public to enter into any obligation relating thereto, to make or disseminate or cause to be made or disseminated before the public in this state, or to make or disseminate or cause to be made or disseminated from this state before the public in any state, in any newspaper or other publication, or any advertising device, or by public outcry or proclamation, or in any other manner or means whatever, including over the Internet, any statement, concerning that real or personal property or those services, professional or otherwise, or concerning any circumstance or matter of fact connected with the propo
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to place links and obtain approval, using 'agrees to generally consider' and 'may attempt to obtain approval.' This creates ambiguity. California Business and Professions Code § 17500, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, makes it 'unlawful... to make or disseminate... any statement, concerning that real or personal property or those services... in any manner or means whatever... any statement, concerning that real or personal property or those services...'. By making the placement of links discretionary, the contract introduces an ambiguity that could mislead consumers about the affiliate's relationship with Chase and the endorsement of their services. This contradicts the law's focus on preventing deceptive advertising, as it makes enforcement and clear consumer understanding less likely.

perturbation.original_text: Affiliate will only use credit card descriptions provided or approved in writing by Chase.
perturbation.changed_text: Affiliate will use credit card descriptions that are generally aligned with those used by Chase.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly states that the affiliate must use descriptions provided or approved by Chase in writing. The modified text introduces ambiguity by stating that the descriptions must be 'generally aligned' with Chase's descriptions. This vague requirement could allow the affiliate to use misleading or inaccurate descriptions that are not explicitly approved by Chase, potentially harming consumers.
perturbation.law_explanation: 15 U.S.C. § 1664 (Fair Credit Reporting Act) addresses advertising of open-end credit plans. By allowing affiliates to use descriptions that are merely 'generally aligned' with Chase's descriptions, there is a risk of disseminating inaccurate or misleading information about the credit card's terms and conditions, such as interest rates, fees, or other charges, which is prohibited under this law. This could deceive consumers into applying for a credit card based on false pretenses.

Advertising of credit other than open end plans (a) Exclusion of open end credit plans Except as provided in subsection (b), this section applies to any advertisement to aid, promote, or assist directly or indirectly any consumer credit sale, loan, or other extension of credit subject to the provisi
perturbation.justification: The original text requires strict adherence to Chase's approved descriptions, while the changed text allows for 'generally aligned' descriptions. This creates an ambiguity. The Law Explanation indicates that the modified text introduces a risk of inaccurate or misleading information about credit card terms, potentially deceiving consumers. The original contract eliminates this risk by explicitly stating the affiliate must use descriptions provided or approved in writing by Chase, insuring accuracy. The changed contract creates an ambiguity that could violate consumer protection laws. Scraped Law Snippet 2 references 15 U.S.C. which is related to the Fair Credit Reporting Act.

perturbation.original_text: Affiliate is solely responsible for ensuring that its reviews and articles obey all applicable copyright and other laws. Generally, Affiliate must have express permission to use another party's copyrighted or other proprietary material.
perturbation.changed_text: Affiliate will endeavor to ensure that its reviews and articles mostly comply with applicable copyright and other laws. Affiliate should attempt to obtain permission to use another party's copyrighted or other proprietary material, where feasible.
perturbation.explanation: The original text imposes a strict obligation on the Affiliate to obey copyright laws and secure explicit permission for copyrighted material usage. The modified version introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation by using the terms 'endeavor to ensure', 'mostly comply', and 'should attempt', which makes the copyright compliance responsibility subjective and not absolute. Additionally, the addition of the term, 'where feasible' makes it uncertain when and if permission from outside parties is needed.
perturbation.law_explanation: 17 U.S.C. § 501 defines copyright infringement and establishes liability for violating copyright laws. By softening the language around copyright compliance ('endeavor to ensure', 'mostly comply', 'should attempt where feasible'), the modified text creates a scenario where the affiliate could potentially infringe on copyrights without facing clear contractual repercussions, thereby contradicting the strict liability principles of copyright law and making enforcement against infringement more challenging.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 U.S. Code § 501 - Infringement of copyright | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 17 U.S. Code § 501 - Infringement of copyright U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Anyone who violates any of the exclusive rights of the copyright owner as provided by sections 106 through 122 or of the author as provided in section 106A(a), or who imports copies or phonorecords into the United States in violation of section 602, is an infringer of the copyright or right of the author, as the case may be. For purposes of this chapter (other than section 506), any reference to copyright shall be deemed to include the rights conferred by section 106A(a). As used in this subsection, the term “ anyone ” includes any State, any instrumentality of a State, and any officer or employee of a State or instrumentality of a State acting in his or her official capacity. Any State, and any such instrumentality, officer, or employee, shall be subject to the provisions of this title in the same manner and to the same extent as any nongovernmental entity. (b) The legal or beneficial owner of an exclusive right under a copyright is entitled, subject to the requirements of section 411, to institute an action for any infringement of that particular right committed while he or she is the owner of it. The court may require such owner to serve written notice of the action with a copy of the complaint upon any person shown, by the records of the Copyright Office or otherwise, to have or claim an interest in the copyright, and shall require that such notice be served upon any person whose interest is likely to be affected by a decision in the case. The court may require the joinder, and shall permit the intervention, of any person having or claiming an interest in the copyright. (c) For any secondary transmission by a cable system that embodies a performance or a display of a work which is actionable as an act of infri
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly weakens the obligation to comply with copyright law, creating a contradiction. The original text states the affiliate 'is solely responsible' and 'must have express permission,' while the modified text uses softer language like 'endeavor to ensure,' 'mostly comply,' and 'should attempt where feasible.' This introduces ambiguity and makes copyright compliance subjective. 17 U.S.C. § 501, as indicated in both snippets, defines copyright infringement and establishes liability for violations. The modified contract language could allow an affiliate to potentially infringe on copyrights without clear contractual repercussions, undermining the strict liability principles inherent in copyright law, as the enforcement becomes more challenging due to the ambiguous language.
